examples.name: Read-only search tool grant
examples.description: A low-risk read-only grant for a code search tool. The agent 'agent-code' can only READ in development and staging environments. No destructive verbs, no approval required.

tool_descriptor.description: Searches across the codebase for patterns and definitions

examples.name: Production write tool requiring approval
examples.description: A high-risk tool grant for editing files in production. The agent 'agent-swe' is allowed WRITE only in production, but it requires human approval. Scope is constrained to src/ directory.

tool_descriptor.description: Creates and modifies files on disk

permission_rule.rationale: Production write requires human approval per change-control policy

authorization_decision.rationale: Production write on edit-file for agent-swe requires human approval before execution

examples.name: Denied access because no matching grant exists
examples.description: An agent tries to use the DELETE verb on the delete-resource tool in production. No grant covers this combination. The default-deny effect applies and the request is rejected.

authorization_decision.rationale: No matching grant found for agent-swe on delete-resource with verb=delete in production. Default-deny applied.

examples.name: Destructive tool descriptor with strict policy
examples.description: A destructive tool that deletes cloud resources. Risk level is CRITICAL. Only the DELETE verb is supported. The tool is marked destructive, which forces approval_required=True on any grant.

tool_descriptor.description: Deletes cloud infrastructure resources (buckets, databases, instances)

examples.note: This grant scopes DELETE to staging only. Even in staging, approval is required because delete is destructive. A production grant for this tool would follow the same pattern but with stricter reviewer requirements via P21.
